reason: Con105 shows that raising the wage can increase a worker's net need for assistance, challenging the claim of saved government spending.
Con106 suggests higher wages may force layoffs, potentially increasing government aid costs, undermining the projected savings.
B claims layoffs will increase reliance on income‑support, challenging the premise that the wage hike will cut $7.6 B in spending.
B argues higher labor cost in low‑cost regions, undermining the assumption that the increase will generate the reported savings.
B says low‑cost area firms cannot raise prices, questioning the premise that the wage rise will reduce government outlays.
B predicts offshoring and outsourcing, which could offset the projected $7.6 B savings, attacking A’s premise.
Con105 claims a higher minimum wage can make a low‑income worker worse off, directly contradicting the implied benefit of pro55’s mandate.
Con106 challenges the feasibility of pro55’s rule by asserting employers cannot afford to keep the same staff at higher wages.
Con106b similarly undermines pro55 by claiming employers would need to lay off workers to meet the higher wage.
con36 shows the rule would impose disproportionate cost burdens on low‑cost‑area employers, challenging the feasibility premise of pro55.
con36b argues low‑cost‑area firms cannot offset higher wages with price hikes, undermining the assumption that the rule is economically viable.
con21 cites CFO intentions to outsource, questioning the economic practicality of pro55’s mandated wage rule.
con105 claims a minimum‑wage increase could make a single mother worse off, directly contradicting pro62’s positive view of giving workers more money.
B argues employers may not afford higher wages, challenging A's premise that corporations can easily give workers more money
B claims layoffs may be needed, undermining A's assumption that corporations have abundant resources
B shows low‑cost‑area employers would bear a larger cost share, questioning A's premise about corporate wealth
B argues customers cannot afford price hikes, further undermining A's premise that corporations can absorb higher wages
B cites CFOs planning offshoring, challenging A’s premise that corporations can easily afford higher wages
B points out a net loss for a single mother, undermining the premise that a higher minimum wage would improve school attendance
Claims tight budgets force employers to cut staff, challenging the premise that higher wages increase household income for schooling.
States layoffs are required to stay within budget, undermining the premise that wage hikes boost family resources for school attendance.
Argues firms cannot raise prices, implying possible job cuts that counteract the income gains needed for school attendance.
Outsourcing and offshoring due to higher wages would reduce domestic employment, undermining the premise of increased family income for schooling.
The con argument shows that a higher minimum wage could reduce a single mother's net income, challenging the premise that higher wages improve family finances and thus lower drop‑out rates.
It argues that employers may have to cut staff due to higher wages, undermining the premise that raising the minimum wage will help keep students in school.
The claim that layoffs are necessary under a higher wage budget attacks the premise that wage hikes improve student outcomes.
It suggests low‑cost‑area firms face higher labor cost ratios, challenging the premise that wage hikes will not harm employment and thus schooling.
The argument that firms cannot raise prices in low‑cost areas implies possible job cuts, undermining the premise linking higher wages to lower drop‑out rates.
It asserts that firms would outsource or move production if wages rise, challenging the premise that higher wages will support student retention.
con105 challenges the premise that higher wages would reduce taxpayer subsidies by showing a higher minimum wage could make a worker financially worse off.
con106 argues that a higher minimum wage forces employers to cut staff, undermining pro27's premise that low wages are the sole cause of taxpayer subsidies.
con106b claims layoffs would be necessary under a higher wage, attacking the premise that low wages alone drive public subsidies.
con36 points out regional cost differences, questioning the uniform $243 billion subsidy estimate used by pro27.
con36b argues low‑cost areas cannot pass higher labor costs to consumers, challenging pro27's premise about the impact of low wages on subsidies.
con21 suggests firms would outsource or contract out if wages rise, undermining pro27's premise that low wages are the primary driver of public subsidies.
B claims higher wages reduce benefit dependence, directly contradicting A’s claim that a wage rise makes a worker worse off.
B states that higher earnings would lower eligibility for benefits, opposing A’s assertion that a wage increase harms the worker financially.
con106b claims layoffs are needed, challenging the premise that low‑income workers will actually earn higher wages.
con36 argues higher wage costs in low‑cost areas, questioning the feasibility of the wage increase assumed by con63.
con36b says employers cannot offset higher labor costs with price hikes, undermining the premise that workers will earn more.
con21 predicts offshoring and outsourcing, casting doubt on the premise that higher wages will be realized for low‑income workers.
con106 states employers cannot keep the same headcount after a wage rise, weakening the premise that workers will earn more.
con106b asserts layoffs are necessary, directly challenging the premise of higher earnings for low‑income workers.
con36 highlights higher cost burdens on employers in low‑cost regions, undermining the feasibility of the wage increase premise.
con36b argues price increases are unaffordable, questioning whether low‑income workers can actually receive higher wages.
Con106 implies higher wages force employers to cut staff, contradicting Con84’s claim that raising the tipped minimum wage won’t cause job losses.
A says minimum‑wage hikes force layoffs, directly contradicting B’s claim that such hikes won’t cause restaurant job losses.
A shows that low‑cost‑area employers would face higher cost burdens, implying layoffs are plausible and thus B’s “no job loss” conclusion does not necessarily follow.
A argues low‑cost‑area firms cannot raise prices to cover higher wages, suggesting layoffs are likely and undercutting B’s claim of no job losses.
